text: Why do coworkers get jealous of people taking vacation time? | I'm relatively new to working in an "adult" job but I've also heard from many others that many times coworkers get jealous when they heard about someone going on vacation, so they don't really share. My question is why? I'm happy for whoever gets to travel. Of course there is some sort of joking jealously like "ahhh girl good for you, I wish I could go too!" I don't have kids or any other responsibilities tying me down, so if I chose to spend my PTO on vacations, how is that a bad thing? Are they jealous that they have to spend their PTO days on family related events?

I got a remote job offer, but they said I have to live in a state away from my home town. I'll be 3 hours from home. I'm excited, but mom doesn't want me to take it because of the distance. How do I continue to feel positive without her approval? | Hi guys! So I am a college senior graduating this month, and I finally found a remote job that offered me a position.  I was super stoked about it, but it is in the state that I'm going to University. I had plans to move home, which is three hours away, but the company said that due to tax laws, I would need to reside in the same state as the job. I feel very grateful to have this opportunity and would be willing to rent an apartment since I live in a cheap area of town. But when I told my mother about the job, she was upset. She actually told me to decline the position and keep looking for a remote job, specifically one that is based in our home town. But it is so difficult to even receive a remote job offer, let alone two. I feel like she's guilt tripping me into staying with her. And sometimes I feel like it's working. I will miss her and my family if I stay in the state I went to university, but my job also said that I could visit for up to 3 weeks at a time, so I will be able to see her. But she's still unhappy. It's really bringing my spirits down. How do I remain positive even after this and prepare to live by myself after college? I would really appreciate your help.

are all or most back-end developer jobs like that? | I'm working with .NET. I'm new in the field so I'm sorry if that makes no sense, but that's a Junior's point of view. 

all I do is copy the same oop boilerplate every time. It's the same thing every day and I feel like it's pretty uninspiring. If I get a task, all I do is make a class, make its controller, service, repository, whatever it's needed, then a migration and voila. 

Unrelated, but linq is a treat and I really like it though. 

I used to hate front-end, but tinkering with front-end has been so far the most fun I've had in my current job. Mostly because you need to come up with things and actually think about what you're doing (in my company's softwares I mean).

Is this the majority of software development jobs? Also, I think my country has fewer 'interesting' jobs since it's a third world country, but I'm not so sure if that makes sense.

if there are jobs that aren't like that, how do I find them?

Am I just weak for wanting a more comfortable, easy job? | Title. I currently work in a very well paying job where we work a 4 day week. Problem is it’s stressful, lots of pressure and expectations.

I don’t care about job titles or being super rich, I just want less stress and enough time to do what I love which is exercise.

Am I just giving up by thinking about moving to a job that’ll be less stressful but also less money? Like somewhere comfortably within my skills where I can make an impact easily.

I know I’ll stop growing in my field but I’m not sure how much I care. Less day to day stress and more time doing things I care about seems more appealing to me.

Am I just being weak and giving up?

What to do if written offer is lower than verbal offer? | Company recruiter verbally offered me the job two weeks ago and asked for my expected salary. I said all I need is for them to match my current and I'll go with them. They said they can match it and will send the written offer soon. A week after that, they called me that unfortunately they can't match my current salary but they'll do their best to come up with a closer number. I thanked them and said that I'm looking forward to the written offer. That written offer came last two days and it is significantly lower than my current salary(35% less). They went over with me the complete package. The other factors of the package were pretty standard. Today, I
I sent them an email if it's still negotiable. They said they'll have to ask another team about it if they can still revise the offer. They also asked me if I will decline if it can't be revised. I didn't answer it directly. Instead, I politely showed them I am interested in the job and willing to work with them to find a beneficial solution for both parties.

Job Searching is hard.. HANG IN THERE. | I'm been extremely grateful in my ability to find a job that'll help me kickstart my career despite me not having a single ounce of experience in my current field... I just wanted to share my story to give hope to others. Please note, the numbers I share are about 95% accurate. 

I got laid off in February from a very cozy warehouse position where i worked off the books and got paid daily. My former boss/warehouse owner decided it would be better to rehire the person i had replaced because he was "forced" by his insurance to hire back a dude who deliberately wished to destroy his livelihood instead of keeping a competent and reliable worker... 

Suffering from depression and having been asked several times by my former employer to come back, i rejected his requests because i refused to work in an environment where i wasn't appreciated even though there were less than 5 total employees and I was one of two warehouse associates. 

I gave himself a week break but i jumped right on the search for jobs. I had updated my resume, created two other versions of my resume with different templates, updated my references, and my skills. I added a STUPID AMOUNT of keywords in white text and left it between the visible text to attempt to fool the algorithms. From the second week of February up until the beginning of July, I made it my new job to wake up every morning at 7am, apply to jobs nonstop from 8AM-4PM every single day. Every single fucking day.
For five and a half months, I was so low and depressed. Between the shitty economy, recession, and my overly populated city thanks to these hundreds of thousands of immigrants, I was struggling to find a job in NYC. Within this time span, I used several screens to constantly apply to any and EVERY SINGLE JOB i saw on Indeed, LinkedIn, ZipRecruiter,  TODAY, Fivver, even Craigslist. I sent out between 5-15 jobs every hour. I made it my goal to do a minimum of 40 applications a day. Within these 5.5 months, I sent over 2,300 applications. This number is a very rough estimate based on the amount of mail i received in my email. The *real* number of applications is certainly over 2,000 applications though.. 

I got a total of 6 interviews. Within those 2,000+ applications in 5.5 months. 4 of these interviews were group interviews that wasted my time as they were pyramid schemes. 1 job rejected me after a formal interview. My current job accepted me after several rounds of interviewing. 
Now, three months after my employment, I'm very happy with my first time office job. I'm comfortable with my starting pay, wonderful benefits including health insurance, plenty of PTO, and a set schedule with my weekends free. Constant office snacks, paid meals biweekly, yearly raises, and the company encourages promoting within before they offer jobs to the general public. I plan to use this position to kickstart my career in the medical industry as a stepping stone, working towards becoming a phlebotomist. 

If you've made it this far, thank you for this lengthy read... I only wrote this to continue to encourage others to focus on that grind to better your employment and better your life. Please do not be discouraged. Please do not give up. I felt like giving up after my first thousand applications. I did quit searching (for about a week) after my second thousand applications. I continued to search because I didn't have a choice and I needed to find a job. The reality is, this world is your oyster. Set a goal for yourself and work hard to secure your position in a proper company that cares about you. Life is uncertain, but the only thing you CAN rely on, is yourself. 

TLDR: Took 5 and a half months and over 2,000 applications before I got a job. Don't give up.
What career / industries are “recession proof”? |  Thinking of switching from tech to something better
Got an internship offer from a company but they are using low-code solution. Is it a time waste or should I do it anyway?

I currently work for a small company. Should I jump ship to a bigger company even though doing so would probably cause my current company to collapse? | I currently work for a small company of about 7 people. It started as an internship right out of college then turned into a job, and in the 5.5 years I've been there I've been promoted up to a VP (everybody gets a fancy job title being a small company). 

However, we've always struggled to make it by and I'm pretty underpaid for the work I do because we're always broke. Since the way the company gets paid is in larger, spread out chunks rather than steady but smaller cash flow, right now only me and two other guys are on salary. The rest are commission-only. 

My boss has promised me multiple times that as soon as we have more money he'll give me a raise, make me a junior partner, etc. and to be fair I don't doubt his intent. He really is a nice guy; he's just not very good at running a business. 

Meanwhile, a very large and well-funded company with whom I work with directly on the weekly basis and have a good relationship with one of their higher-ups is hiring for a position that's right up my alley and that I qualify for. They have great benefits, I'd get to work remote, and even the low end of the posted salary range is 50% more than what I'm making now. 

Logically, it makes all the sense in the world to make the move. 

On the other hand, I'm currently in a position with my company where I'm in charge of all the day-to-day stuff and there are many critical functions that only I'm trained to do and manage. If I left there's a good chance the company would collapse and everyone else there would lose their jobs. 

I feel like my head is telling me one thing and my heart another; I need some advice.

Is quant really the only lucrative career options for someone with strong math and coding skills? | Do I have any hopes to get into quant as a new grad? Is there anything else I can do besides FAANG?

I finished undergrad in CS in 2 years at a not amazing state school and am finishing my masters at a T10 in June 2024 (which is when I’m supposed to finish my undergrad).

During that time I didn’t do the classical FAANG internships. Instead I have some data science work experience at a small company, machine learning research experience (decent but not great), and about a year of full time SWE experience at a F100 company. I’m making 125k but the quant HFT options seem to be much more lucrative. 

I’m obviously not getting into Jane street or citadel or high tier firms, but how do I get an interview for other possibly less known quant firms?

Ideally I would be looking for a quant research or trader role that pays 300k TC. 

Since my experience/education doesn’t look good on its own, should I try to highlight it on my resume so it looks better that I did these in a normal college timeline/4 years?

My biggest question is where do I apply? I feel like I exhausted my options with 2 sigma,  Jane Street, Aquatic, HRT, SIG. all of these pay a lot but where can I find quant researcher roles that pay a little less and will not auto reject me? (Most of those above did, some didn’t). 

I think  I would be good at this but if I don’t get in anywhere now, I won’t get in later, so where should I apply and how to find these??

What else can I do besides quant?

Chinese manager speaking Chinese around me and talking negatively about Americans | I work for an American company but my broader team is about 95% Chinese. The team was built by a Chinese person and almost everyone immigrated from China. On my immediate team, I‘m the only non-chinese person. I was actually hired by a different manager but due to a re-org I was moved to this team.   


I don’t think my manager likes me and she has made several offensive comments regarding ‘American people.‘ Some people were having a potluck and she commented that Americans are so relaxed and dont do enough and Chinese people work so much harder etc and that our team is so much brighter and more intelligent because there are so many smart Chinese immigrants. And other people on the team have made derogatory comments about white coworkers saying that they’re just ‘native American speakers’ who don’t know how to do anything but talk.

​

A few times my manager will be speaking Chinese to others on the team during meetings which I can’t understand and act annoyed when I join and they have to switch English.   


I’m treated as an outsider on my team and my manager is cold and dismissive towards me. I was a top performer on my previous team but I think my manager sees American people as not being bright enough and treats me as an outsider.   


I have to stick it out at this job for at least 6 more months so I don’t lose out on bonus, but it’s a super frustrating situation and I’m not sure how to handle it. I’m not sure if it’s just Chinese culture but it sucks to be talked down to all the time and treated like an idiot.  


​

Is quitting a high stress job for a lower paying job/less stressful job worth it? | TL.DR.: Stress and anxiety is causing performance issues at work and potentially being fired. Is a step down in job duties/pay grade within the company better than finding a low paying job elsewhere? 

I’m a 34 year old guy who works in the insurance industry as a commercial underwriter. I moved up within the company to this current position about a year ago there have been some growing pains that are stressing me out to the point where I am having panic attacks. I’ve been told I will be put in disciplinary action at work if I don’t turn it around soon. 

I want to try and get better at my job, but I just feel like I’m at the point of no return, where I want to move on now. 

I’m ashamed of myself for even trying to better myself, and my depression/ptsd is only making things worse. 

So my question is, would a lower paying job be worth it for me? Would moving into a lower position within the company make sense? 

I want to move to web design, but I feel like I don’t have the energy anymore to take on more things. I feel so lost, and I’m just freaking out now, I don’t want to let anyone down, but my mental health is suffering so much because of this. Any advice at all would be so helpful. Thanks for reading!

Where is this magical job market people are talking about? | Husband and I have both been interviewing for remote jobs for months. He has lots of experience, has been getting interviews for good jobs, having interviewers gush over him, nothing. I have less experience but have had 10 interviews for relevant jobs, think I’m doing well, rejected. What’s going on here? It feels like 2008. What more can they want? And who are these people who are getting remote jobs at the drop of a hat? It has stressed me out because my current work environment is becoming extremely abusive but I’m essentially a wage slave until I can find a way to get out of here. I am also seeing the same jobs posted over and over and over again….even with the new year.

Edit: thanks for the comments, support, and advice everyone. I’m trying to take my frustration and put it towards sending more applications next week. I guess the fact I’m even getting interviews has to be kind of promising. It’s tempting to look for anything with how awful my boss is but at this point I’ll widen my net a little with remote but will not take anything in office that I know would make me really unhappy. Just gonna take my time, worst case scenario I have more experience on my resume from being stuck here and I become the magical candidate lol.

I just realized I've already given up | I've been looking for a job for about 3 years now. During university and a about 6 months after graduating, I did the stereotypical applying exclusively to the big name companies in my industry and rightfully so, got rejected. For the next 6 months, I tried the smaller companies and unpaid positions in my area. Writing personalized cover letters, getting my resumes reviewed by recruiters and industry professionals and my portfolio reworked, etc. Still nothing. At that point, I thought I needed something. I applied pretty much anywhere i could while still trying to get into my industry, I did the spreadsheet listing where I applied and when to keep track, trying to keep organized and realized I had gotten no interviews.

I did my year, getting some unofficial udemy certifications and all that and then found out that I could see how many ppl had viewed my portfolio and the likes. After 2 years at that point and 400+ applications, I had about 16 views, most of which had to have been from myself and the reviewers. The rejections were already getting to me but I think realing no one was even looking at them really put me over the edge. I completely gave up on my degree and started applying for anything I knew even remotely anything about. I tried networking and reaching out to recruiters, I had direct ins to a few entry level positions in a few companies where I've been directly recommended by ppl that work at the company and still have been rejected. Im not above nepotism and having parents directly recommend me me and have still been rejected.

I finally caved and applied for mcdonalds. Imagine my surprise when they rejected me twice. I started mass applying for retail and food service and still nothing, not even interviews. I think about 6 months ago I stopped keeping track of where I was applying to in my big Google sheets doc, I stopped changing my resume for each job, I stopped writing cover letters completely, and now I barely remember where I apply to. I feel like a machine. I dont even know whats going on anymore. I picked up some bad habits from university, mainly being able to ignore pretty much everything in order to finish a task including ignoring hunger, but now I dont have deadlines and almost 3 years later I eat like 1 meal a day at like 2am, all just so I can apply to a position I know im not going to get anyway.

I used to watch like motivational stuff and I think it was like The Rock that said "if you want to get bigger, make the gym part of your daily routine even if you don't workout just go. Eventually you will start working out since you are already there" I think that's where I'm at for the job search. I'm not really sure where I'm applying at this point. I kind of just wake up, sit at my cpu, and 6 hours later I regain consciousness with like 4 new applications sent out. Its just part of my daily routine now.

I only realized today because I got en email application confirmation from a company I thought was a scam for the past like 6 years that I told myself I would never applied to. I didnt even realize I applied. I dont want to give up but I dont see myself going back to writing new resumes or cover letters for every position. I really don't know what to do anymore.

After multiple interviews, I’m told the salary range they listed was “incorrect”. What do I do? | I applied for this role in July. It was listed as 65-75K - in desired salary, I indicated I needed 75 (it wouldn’t make sense for me to switch jobs if not)

When I had my first round screening, they confirmed with me that the range of this role was ok with me.

Fast forward a month, I’ve gone through all my interviews and am allegedly receiving my offer this week. I got a call today just telling me I will specifically hear on Wednesday so to prepare my references.

In this call, the HR lady told me “there’s been some mix up on our end” and the role is a flat 65K salary… HUH? She claims it was a mistake and the listing is wrong. I will add also that all roles of this level have this salary listed.

She sounded very uncomfortable. Obviously I am kind of pissed. I told her that I find it a bit disappointing  that there was not accurate pay transparency and that the salary was a driving factor in my applying. She said she gets it and we can discuss more once I receive the offer.

I’m not taking this role if that is what I am offered, I feel like they knowingly wasted my time and I don’t appreciate that. Is this grounds to wager for 70-75 as it’s what was advertised at all steps of the process?

How to fix negative attitude? | I hope I titled my question correctly, had troubles phrasing my thoughts. 

My last job left me very burnt out, and I realized after working there I became very angry, easily irritated and not that open to communication.   
My workplace was never clear with their tasks and very disorganized, my attempts at communication were always brushed off. Their way of operating always ended up with tons of revisions - one revision after another after another. You fix one thing, they tell you change another sentence, you save the files and send them out and what comes next? Another revision, this time change color of one small thing. Then it keeps going and going, they fix one thing at a time. Sometimes they'd tell me to change things from months ago that I *knew* were bad idea but they always insisted on doing it.  
So with time I came to dread the revisions. I tried to communicate but was brushed off. And as a result, at some point all the revisions made me feel very irritated. I started to lash out on people (mostly being cold or telling them I will get to revisions after I finish other tasks first, or doing only revisions they mentioned and if they missed out something - I'd tell them they never told me to do that, which they didn't like).

I'm scared this negative attitude will follow me to next workplace, so I'm asking for advice. How do I fix my attitude?

I suffer from being a generalist with no marketable skill set | TLDR: I have a software degree with experience in web development, design, and digital marketing (and a lot in-between). I know how to do a lot of things somewhat well. I'm very good at picking things up on the spot, but I definitely lack deep knowledge in any one single area. Now, in this job market, I'm struggling to find anything.

Small businesses love me, but I can't get a decent-paying job at a decent company for the life of me. I graduated with a bachelor's in Software Development over 4 years ago but had trouble finding a job afterward. I landed myself into a somewhat technical project at a non-technical company, but I was bored and wanted more, so I went part-time with them and started my own website agency having never built a full website in my life.

Honestly, it didn't go so bad. This is when I learned that I also have a knack for web design, so I built a couple websites for a couple of small businesses before picking up a contract-to-hire remote Junior Web Developer role. I learned a ton and actually successfully finished a lot of tickets with no pushback. But I got covid for a couple of weeks, and then came back and fucked up a merge, so they decided to end the contract two months in. I later learned from my recruiter that they did the same thing to several other contract-to-hires in different departments. Who knows, maybe she just wanted me to feel better.

I must be great at selling myself because, soon after, I somehow landed a role as a senior-level UI/UX Designer at a local company (they liked my website designs that much I guess). I thought things were going great. I helped them adopt Figma and increase the accessibility of their customer portal. But they "laid me off" after a month because they "weren't able to secure funding for the position". Who knows. Again, I think these people just want to make me feel better.

I found a few more clients after that to help keep us afloat. I created more design systems on Figma, created a couple more websites, and taught myself different CMSs. I picked up a Shopify client and we hit it off. We decided to make it a permanent full-time position where I would continue to manage and optimize the website, as well as lead 100% of our digital marketing efforts. What I later learned is that their business model wasn't sustainable. They were in a space where they profited big during covid, but they had practically zero profit margin and orders had started to plummet before I even came on. I take full responsibility for not recognizing this earlier. They eventually realized this too, so they pivoted  the company and laid me off. I actually still contract with them to manage their website, but now I'm back to ground zero.

So now what? I have a software degree with this broad, yet non-marketable skill set. Small companies like to hire generalists (like my last role) because they can't afford full teams. Larger companies look at my doozy of a resume and pass. For any positions I got laid off from, I just put that experience under my freelancing business. 

I like being a generalist because I love to learn how everything and wear a lot of hats, but I have a wife and three kids and need a sustainable income. Has anyone else had a successful career doing what I do? What helped you in your career? If not, maybe it's time I lean into one area and embrace it.

Is it appropriate for me to write an SOP off the clock? | So, my boss has asked me to write a brand new SOP for my position from the ground up. The actual work duties of my specific position have changed over the past few years, and I've only been there the past few months but, and he's looking to rework some of the employment positions with promotions and transfers.

Generally, my boss is actually been pretty great. He's a strong proponent that you should (mostly) leave your personal life at the door, but leave your work life at work when you leave. For the most part, the only thing he expects work related outside of the clock is communicating about scheduling since hours can be in flux date to day. Which, doesn't at all sound unreasonable to me.

But, given my previous history with some really bad employers, I wanted to know if it would be appropriate for me to work on rewriting this SOP outside of work, especially when he openly said that he just wants me to try to add a few lines every week when I have a few minutes between tasks.

Lesson Learned: That person everyone hates at work is hated for a reason. Even if they're c-suite level. | I started working at a new position awhile back and noticed that someone in our c-suite is particularly disliked at work, let's call him Ron. For a long time I thought people were honestly being mean to Ron, and truthfully, people at all levels from intro to fellow c-suite members are pretty rude and dismissive of Ron, to the point that I've questioned why he still works here. 

A: If  they don't want to listen to him, or take his suggestions seriously then why not hire someone else? 

and, 

B: I honestly don't know how he puts up with it. Everyone low-key shits on him. 

So when I started noticing this, I decided to try to be nice to Ron as best as I can. He's not rude, just exceptionally chatty on pretty boring topics (he's the CFO after all). I found out he had been divorced and just lives at home with some cats, and so I honestly kinda felt for the guy because he clearly doesn't have anyone to talk to. So whenever he had something to say I tried to give him my attention, and I've always followed through on the smile and nod whenever he would yack away and I put genuine effort into trying to make him feel at least somewhat heard at work, because I definitely know what that feels like. Well. Now I know why everyone hates this guy. 

I figured it never hurts to be on the good side of the folks in the c-suite. But apparently all I've accomplished here is being the nail that sticks out for Ron. He now feels the need to provide input and suggestions for my work (literally not a thing to do with his department), and has even complained about my work and delivery. Talking to Ron just put me on his radar as someone he can complain about at work, so yeah, that's the last time I'll make that mistake. My coworkers are petty for sure, and honestly most of them are pretty mean spirited about Ron, but I guess I know now why literally no one likes Ron and gives him constant passive aggressive BS. Lesson learned ya'll. Now I've got this c-suite level guy whose been there since dirt breathing down my neck on stuff, and I was already thinking about leaving/applying because my role is exceptionally underpaid, (if I didn't have assets from selling my business I'd qualify for affordable housing), but goddamn it's brutal job hunting out there.

But yeah, if there's someone at work that everyone hates, even if your workplace is kind of petty, there's still a reason for that.
